Multi-Site Solar Irradiance Forecasting

Report generated: 2026-04-17  |  Models: GRUGNN  |  Forecast horizons: 1h, 6h, 24h

Dataset

Hourly solar irradiance data from the NSRDB (National Solar Radiation Database) for five stations in Catalonia and northeast Spain. The target is station 401390 (lat 41.93°N, lon 2.26°E); the four surrounding stations are used as spatial context (neighbor inputs). Data covers three full years (2017–2019). The chronological split reserves the first 70% for training, the next 15% for validation (hyperparameter tuning & early stopping), and the final 15% as the held-out test set.

What is the Persistence baseline?
The persistence model is the simplest possible forecast: it assumes the clearsky index (kt) at the prediction time equals the most recent observed value — i.e.  kt̂[t+h] = kt[t]. It requires no training and serves as the standard reference in solar forecasting. A model that cannot beat persistence is not useful.

Skill Score measures how much better a model is compared to persistence: Skill = 1 − RMSEmodel / RMSEpersistence. A score of 0 means identical to persistence; 1 means perfect. In the time-series plots below, the persistence line is always shown as a dashed grey line.
How the lookback window works
At each hourly timestep t, both models receive the past 24 hours of observations as input (the grey-shaded band in the time-series plots marks this window for the first prediction point in the test set). From that window the model outputs three simultaneous forecasts: kt at t+1h, t+6h, and t+24h. The window then slides forward by one hour for the next prediction — none of the test-period target values are ever seen during training.

Model Architectures

SolarGRU — multi-branch Gated Recurrent Unit

SolarGNN — Graph Convolutional Network + GRU

Results in W/m² — Global Horizontal Irradiance

Conversion from kt to GHI (W/m²)
GHI = kt × Clearsky GHI
The Clearsky GHI at each target timestep is taken directly from the NSRDB clear-sky model column, which is deterministically known for any future hour from solar geometry alone. Multiplying the model's kt output by this value yields an actual irradiance forecast in watts per square metre.
